title: Is the scientific enterprise too risk-averse?
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Aggressive fish mellow out on shrooms, Solar System’s largest moon is unique to its core, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
7 May 2026
Today’s SciencePrudence recounts a lively debate about risk in research. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including living, glowing materials and how ‘magic’ mushrooms help aggressive fish chill.
Astronomy  |  News from Science
Solar System’s largest moon is unique to its core
In 1996, NASA’s Galileo spacecraft made a shocking discovery at Ganymede, Jupiter’s—and the Solar System’s—largest moon. Unlike every other known moon, it hosted an internally powered magnetic field called a dynamo. Now, scientists think the reason might make the moon doubly unique: its core may still be forming.

“I’m used to thinking of core formation being this ancient process,” said first author Kevin Trinh. But the new work “suggests that core formation might be delayed by billions of years.”

Typically, the elements within a planet or moon stratify over time, with heavier elements like iron sinking down to form a dense core. When that core churns, it generates electrical currents that sustain a magnetic field. Scientists only know of such dynamos on the gas giants, Earth, and Mercury. But when researchers modeled the process on Ganymede to match the magnetic field measurements from Galileo, they found that the core might not simply be churning, but actively growing, with new iron still dripping down from rocks in the moon’s mantle. If true, that would render Ganymede the only known body still to be forming its center.

Scientists may gain some clarity in 5 years’ time, when the European Space Agency’s JUICE spacecraft arrives at Ganymede. If their hypothesis is true, “that will make [Ganymede] even more special,” Trinh said.
Read the Science Advances paper
Read the full story
Biochemistry  |  Science Advances
Materials made of bioluminescent algae keep on glowing on
An acidic solution illuminates this pattern made of bioluminescent algae, 3D printed to resemble the Colorado Buffaloes logo.  Giulia Brachi/CU Boulder
If you venture down to the beach at night, you might see clouds of bioluminescent algae twinkling like stars in the surf. The flashes of blue light produced by species like Pyrocystis lunula can be triggered by the motion of crashing waves or splashing feet; they typically last only a few milliseconds. Now, the authors of a new study have found a way to keep the glow going, creating living materials that emit light without the need for electricity.

Scientists exposed P. lunula to different chemical solutions, one as acidic as tomato juice and another that was highly basic. Both conditions made the algae glimmer; the glow from the basic solution was diffuse and fleeting, while the acidic solution produced bright, concentrated light that lasted as long as 25 minutes. “It was a very exciting moment when we found the right chemical stimulant that allowed the light to stay on for a long time ,” lead study author Giulia Brachi said in a statement. The team embedded the algae into a water-based gel, which they 3D printed into a variety of shapes and structures. These materials proved remarkably durable, with the algae staying alive and retaining much of their brightness even after several weeks.

In the future, the researchers wrote, such bioluminescent structures could aid water quality assessments by lighting up in the presence of toxins or help robots explore environments in the deep sea and space—no batteries required. Because P. lunula is photosynthetic, these materials could even do all that while sucking up carbon.
Read the paper
Animal Behavior  |  Frontiers in Behavioral Neuroscience
Aggressive fish mellow out on shrooms
The mangrove rivulus fish is tiny but mighty. These fascinating creatures can tolerate an impressive range of salinities, are mostly hermaphrodites that reproduce through self-fertilization, and can survive for up to 2 months on dry land—where they move around by jumping. They are also highly aggressive towards members of their own species, making them notoriously unruly tank-mates. But when exposed to a key ingredient from so-called ‘magic’ mushrooms, these combative critters get way more mellow.

As part of a new study, scientists placed some of these fish in a water tank with dissolved psilocybin, the psychoactive compound produced by more than 200 species of mushrooms. In mammals, this chemical binds to serotonin receptors in the brain, often causing effects like euphoria and hallucinations. After a fish was exposed to the substance for 20 minutes, researchers transferred it to a different tank, which was already occupied by a second fish. Thanks to a fiberglass mesh barrier, the two fish could see and smell, but not reach each other.

Fish that had been exposed to psilocybin performed fewer swimming bursts—a type of aggressive attack behavior—toward their neighbors. The treatment didn’t have much of an effect, however, on other types of aggressive social behavior, which require less energy. “We really did not expect it to just target that highly aggressive and more energetically costly behavior, rather than the low-energy behaviors,” lead study author Dayna Forsyth explained to 404 Media. “That was definitely a surprise.”

Drugged fish also got a lot lazier, spending less time moving around the tank than their clean counterparts. Forsyth said future experiments could dig into why only certain behaviors were affected. “We really had no idea what we were getting ourselves into,” Forsyth said, as there is little research on the effects of drugs on fish. “It would be interesting to increase [the] dose and see if it almost reverses the effects.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what’s possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
SciencePrudence
From left: Brandon Ogbunu, Tyler Cowen, moderator John Donvan, Kate Biberdorf, and Sethuraman Panchanathan  J. Mervis/Science
‘Is the scientific enterprise too risk-averse?’
Jeffrey Mervis, Senior Correspondent, News from Science
That age-old question was the topic of a lively debate this week at Johns Hopkins University that generated some verbal fireworks—as well as a surprising consensus on what’s needed to regain public trust in science and weather the Trump administration’s assaults on academic research.

Each side featured a policy heavyweight. George Mason University economist Tyler Cowen, whose 2011 best-seller The Great Stagnation argued that technological progress has slowed in recent decades, took the affirmative position. The former director of the National Science Foundation (NSF) , Sethuraman Panthanathan, vociferously defended the performance of NSF and other federal research agencies in bankrolling risky ideas. Cowen partnered with Yale University computational biologist and ecologist Brandon Ogbunu, and Panchanathan was flanked by chemist and science popularizer Kate Biberdorf of the University of Notre Dame.

Ogbunu led off the debate and came out swinging. “Science is a little like a circus, and much more like a fiefdom, where rituals, power, and hierarchy dictate the way things are done,” he asserted. “And like fiefdoms, science has an aversion to risk, a lack of creativity, an inability to experiment.”

Panchanathan counterpunched immediately, citing his former agency as a prime example. “There is no evidence to say that somehow we have become risk-averse,” argued Panchanathan, who resigned from NSF 3 months after President Donald Trump, who chose him in 2019, returned to the White House. “The granting process that we use today, which has not changed, gives a lot of empowerment to program officers. And they do an unbelievable of job investing in high-risk products, like NSF’s CAREER awards for young investigators.”

Although he was passionate about his former agency, Panchanathan has studiously avoided commenting on anything that has happened at NSF since his departure, including terminated grants, a shrunken staff, a major reorganization, the absence of a Senate-confirmed director, the firing of its oversight body, and an unexpected headquarters move. “I did all I could while I was there,” he told Science Adviser after the debate. “But I prefer to look ahead. I don’t think it’s my place to give advice to the current leadership.”

In making their arguments, each side accused the other of leaning on anecdotes rather than evidence. However, every debater cited individuals whose careers buttressed their points.

For Cowen it was Katalin Kariko, the 2023 Nobel laureate in medicine whose with mRNA spurred the development of a vaccine against SARS-CoV-2. “NIH took too long and was afraid of taking risks,” Cowen said in claiming that the country’s leading biomedical research agency had to be prodded in early 2020 to shorten the timeframe for reviewing grant proposals to combat the COVID pandemic. He also cited studies showing that research productivity, as measured by patents and new products, has slowed in the last 50 years despite a huge increase in the number of scientists.

In his rebuttal, Panchanathan touted NSF’s early support for Jennifer Doudna, co-developer of the gene-splicing CRISPR technique that netted her a Nobel Prize in 2020. NSF has funded some 270 Nobel Prize winners over its 76-year history, he noted, adding that “science ministers from around the world would ask me, ‘How can we create an NSF in our country?’ The data speaks for itself.”

Biberdorf referenced a Notre Dame colleague who switched fields after his wife’s 12-week regimen of chemotherapy to fight breast cancer “kicked her ass,” so he pivoted to develop a better way of tracking cancer tumors that could reduce the amount of chemotherapy needed. “He works at a university, so he was able to start working on this problem immediately,” Biberdorf said. “And I would argue that universities have both the opportunity and responsibility [to allow faculty] to take these risks.”

However, Ogbunu pointed to his own experience in arguing the opposite—that choosing to do something outside their area of expertise can jeopardize a researcher’s chances of earning tenure. “I was told by everybody in my department not to do it,” he said. The so-called “pivot penalty” is well-documented, Ogbunu said.

The participants never actually agreed on a definition of risk. “Risk is not irresponsibility … or sloppy science … or a disbelief in science,” Ogbunu asserted. But it’s hard to pin down, he admitted. “It’s a matter of questioning your practices, your rituals and your incentives, and then, if they don’t work, you change them.”

Panchanathan’s attempt at a definition was similarly fuzzy. “We want bold but not bad ideas,” he said. “NSF is not risk-averse, but it’s risk-aware.”

Cowen said that it’s an article of faith among economists that large organizations are risk-averse by nature and that U.S. science—which includes academic institutions, industry, and philanthropy as well as state and federal governments—is no exception. Despite his critique, however, his proposed solution to the problem was music to the ears of his debate opponents.

“The truly risky thing for our establishment to be doing would be to expand support for science across all these sectors,” Cowen said in his closing argument. “We may disagree on exactly how to do it best. But is that what America is doing in 2026? I just do not flat out see that.”

Nor does Panchanathan. “Science funding should be a lot, lot more than what it is right now,” he agreed, adding that “that’s one thing that we fought for every day when I was at NSF.”

There was also consensus that scientists need to get better at educating the public about the role of risk-taking—and how failure can be important as success—in trying to solve pressing societal problems. “I’ve never met a single scientist who doesn’t have that ‘Moon shot’ idea that they want to go after. It is part of our culture,” said Biberdorf, evoking nods from the other participants. “But scientists need to get out of our jargon jungle and talk to the public about the risks that they are taking every single day in the lab trying to make this world a better place.”

The debate was the sixth in a series sponsored by the Stavros Niarchos Foundation at Johns Hopkins and Open to Debate, a New York nonprofit whose goal is to “restore critical thinking … and civility to public discourse.” Toward that end, the audience was asked to take a stance—yes, no, or undecided—before and after the 75-minute debate.

Although the organizers view the vote as simply a way to encourage engagement, the results suggest that Cowen and Ogbunu can claim victory. Their affirmative side widened its initial lead, jumping from 42% to 55%, while those arguing no inched up by half that amount, from 21% to 27%. The undecideds shrunk by more than half, with two-thirds joining Cowen and Ogbunu’s camp.
Et Cetera
The universe through keyholes
Astronomers have come up with a seemingly counterintuitive solution to get better resolution from JWST: blocking much of the light from its giant mirror. They’ve been able to combine several keyholelike views into a crisper image using interferometry. “I’m very, very glad that it [worked],” said one of the team members. “It was definitely not pleasant for a while there.”
Publications of the Astronomical Society of Australia Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Unconsciously aware
People under general anesthesia aren’t completely oblivious to the world around them. Deep in their brains, a structure called the hippocampus remains alert, researchers have found. Though they cannot remember or feel pain, they can parse spoken phrases and even predict what someone will say next. “The brain has developed such amazing, sophisticated mechanisms for doing all these complex tasks all day long, that it can do some of these things even without us being aware,” one expert said.
Nature Paper  |  Read more at Nature
Sing with me
Alston’s singing mice certainly earned that moniker. They serenade one another with adorable chirps, but what’s especially intriguing about them is that they never duet: One mouse always waits for its partner to finish before singing back. Their complex conversations appear to stem from “a relatively subtle change in brain wiring,” as one researcher put it—one that wasn’t even noticeable until the team used a technique that maps the connections of individual neurons. Similar changes could have set our own species up for sophisticated speech.
Nature Paper  |  Read more at The New York Times
I was ravenous for prestige, lunging at every award, every travel grant, and every fellowship as if they were life rafts … I wasn’t just building a career; I craved validation from a system that, much like my father, was never quite satisfied.
Working Life  |  30 April 2026  |  Tomasz Głowacki
Last but not least
It’s not AI’s superhuman abilities that have me worried; it’s the way that they sometimes behave all too much like humans in their worst moments.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter and Phie Jacobs

items.title: its core may still be forming
creating living materials that emit light without the need for electricity
these combative critters get way more mellow
lively debate this week at Johns Hopkins University
they sometimes behave all too much like humans in their worst moments

raw_metadata.subject: Is the scientific enterprise too risk-averse?
